title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Priya has told you she wants to start her own business but does not know where to begin. Write an email to Priya giving her practical advice on how to get started, explaining what she should research and plan before launching, and encouraging her to pursue her ambition.

Write your email to Priya.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Priya,

How brilliant — I am so excited to hear that you are thinking of starting your own business! It can feel incredibly daunting at first, but with the right preparation, it is absolutely achievable. Here is what I would suggest.

Before anything else, spend time thoroughly researching your market. Who are your potential customers? Who are your competitors, and what do they offer? Understanding the landscape you are entering is absolutely essential before you invest any time or money.

Next, write a simple business plan. It does not need to be long or complicated, but it should set out your idea clearly, identify your target audience, outline your pricing strategy, and include a basic financial projection. Many free templates are available online.

I would also strongly recommend speaking to someone who has already done it. Your local enterprise office often offers free mentoring sessions with experienced business owners, which is genuinely invaluable.

Finally — believe in yourself. The fact that you are thinking about this seriously puts you ahead of most people who simply dream.

I cannot wait to hear how it develops!

With all the best,
Finn

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read a magazine article suggesting that young people today have more opportunities than ever to start businesses. Write a letter to the magazine editor responding to this claim, describing the real challenges young entrepreneurs face, and suggesting what more could be done to support them.

Write your letter to the editor.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

Your recent feature on young entrepreneurship painted an encouraging picture, and I do not dispute that opportunities exist. However, I feel the article significantly underestimated the very real challenges that young people face when attempting to start a business, and I would like to offer a more balanced perspective.

Access to funding remains one of the most significant barriers. Young entrepreneurs typically lack the credit history, assets, or collateral that banks require before approving business loans. Many rely instead on family support — an option that is simply not available to those from less affluent backgrounds, creating an immediate and unfair inequality of opportunity.

Beyond finance, young entrepreneurs frequently lack access to mentoring and professional networks that more established business owners take for granted. The knowledge gaps this creates can be difficult to bridge independently.

Governments and educational institutions could do considerably more — by integrating entrepreneurship education into secondary school curricula, creating accessible grant schemes specifically for young founders, and establishing free mentoring programmes in every region.

Opportunity without support is not enough.

Yours sincerely,
A Young Entrepreneur

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'The freedom of self-employment versus the security of a regular job — which would you choose and why? Write an article exploring the pros and cons of being self-employed compared to working for a company.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Your Own Boss — Dream or Nightmare?

The image of the self-employed professional — working flexible hours, pursuing passion projects, answering to no one — is enormously appealing. But how does it compare to the reality, and is it really preferable to the security of a salaried position?

Self-employment undeniably offers freedoms that traditional employment cannot match. The ability to set your own schedule, choose your clients, and build something genuinely your own are powerful motivations. Many self-employed people report higher job satisfaction than they experienced as employees.

However, the financial realities deserve honest scrutiny. Income is unpredictable, particularly in the early years. There are no sick pay entitlements, no pension contributions from an employer, and no guaranteed holiday pay. The administrative burden of managing accounts, taxes, and insurance falls entirely on the individual.

Working for a company, by contrast, provides structure, colleagues, mentoring, and the psychological safety of a regular income — benefits that are easy to undervalue until they are absent.

The right choice depends entirely on your personality, your financial resilience, and your appetite for risk. Neither path is universally superior — both require honest self-assessment.
